text.title: Gcp Iam Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules for the gcp-iam-design methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale + concrete remediation hook.

rule.statement: Cross-cutting IAM bindings (security viewer, billing user, network admin) MUST be applied at folder level and inherited, never per-project.
rule.rationale: Per-project bindings drift; folder-level bindings stay consistent and auditable.

rule.statement: Use predefined roles tailored to the action, not basic roles (`Owner`, `Editor`, `Viewer`) on production resources.
rule.rationale: Basic roles grant excessive permissions and violate least-privilege.

rule.statement: External CI/CD (GitHub Actions, GitLab) MUST use Workload Identity Federation; long-lived service-account JSON keys are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Static SA keys are the largest single source of GCP credential leaks; WIF eliminates the static credential entirely.

rule.statement: Sensitive bindings (Owner, IAM admin, Secret Manager admin) MUST carry IAM Conditions restricting resource, time, or source IP where applicable.
rule.rationale: Conditions narrow exposure of the most powerful roles without losing operational flexibility.

rule.statement: Developers and tooling MUST impersonate service accounts via short-lived tokens, never download SA JSON keys.
rule.rationale: Impersonation is auditable and revocable; downloaded keys leak in repos, dotfiles, and incident drives.

rule.statement: If the input fails ALL "Applies If" preconditions or matches ANY "Skip If" disqualifier, do not apply this methodology — route the task to the adjacent sibling instead.
rule.rationale: Even flat checklists need an explicit skip leaf in the decision tree; this rule is the universal terminal.
